# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define **Fast Prototyping** in the context of VLSI verification. List any two advantages it offers over traditional simulation-based verification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fast Prototyping is a verification methodology in which the synthesizable RTL design is realized on reconfigurable hardware platforms such as **FPGAs** or **hardware emulators** to achieve real-time or near-real-time functional execution. It enables the design to be exercised at hardware speeds (typically MHz to hundreds of MHz), allowing real software workloads, operating systems, and peripheral interfaces to be validated long before silicon fabrication.

**Advantages over simulation (1 mark — any two):**
1. Execution speeds are **1000× to 1,000,000× faster** than software simulation, enabling the running of real OS, firmware, and applications.
2. Allows **in-circuit testing** with actual peripheral devices, providing realistic system-level validation.
3. Facilitates **early software development**, allowing software teams to begin integration 6–12 months before tape-out.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is **100% testing** (exhaustive testing)? Why is it practically infeasible for large VLSI designs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** 100% testing, also called *exhaustive testing*, is a verification approach in which **every possible combination of input vectors** is applied to the circuit-under-test and the corresponding outputs are verified. For a combinational circuit with $n$ primary inputs, the test set size is exactly $2^n$ vectors.

**Infeasibility reasoning (2 marks):** The number of test vectors grows **exponentially** with the number of inputs. For a circuit with $n = 64$ inputs, the test set contains $2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ vectors. Even at a vector application rate of $10^9$ vectors per second, the total test time would exceed **580 years**. Hence, for any industrial-scale VLSI design, exhaustive testing is **mathematically and economically infeasible**, and must be replaced by sampled, randomized, or formal methods.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **methodology of Fast Prototyping** for a complex SoC design. Discuss the typical design flow from RTL to FPGA realization and the key challenges encountered in multi-FPGA partitioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A combinational multiplier has $n = 24$ primary inputs. The simulation environment applies one test vector every $t_{\text{vec}} = 50\ \text{ns}$. Calculate the total time required for 100% testing. If the same design is deployed on an FPGA prototype running at $f_{\text{clk}} = 100\ \text{MHz}$ and is exercised with the same workload, what is the speedup factor? Comment on the practical feasibility of each approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Fast Prototyping Methodology (7 marks)

**1. RTL-to-FPGA Design Flow (3 marks):**
The Fast Prototyping flow consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **RTL Preparation:** The synthesizable HDL (Verilog/SystemVerilog/VHDL) is refined to be FPGA-friendly. This includes removing asynchronous constructs, replacing vendor-specific cells with FPGA primitives, and adding clock/reset infrastructure.
2. **Logic Synthesis:** Commercial tools (e.g., **Synopsys Synplify**, **Xilinx Vivado**, **Intel Quartus**) map the RTL onto the FPGA's primitive library (LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs).
3. **Partitioning (for multi-FPGA designs):** If the design exceeds a single FPGA's capacity, the netlist is partitioned across multiple FPGAs using cut-based algorithms. Cut signals are time-multiplexed using MUX logic to fit within the available inter-FPGA pin budget.
4. **Place & Route:** The synthesized netlist is physically placed and routed on the FPGA fabric, generating the bitstream.
5. **Bitstream Loading & Validation:** The bitstream is loaded onto the FPGA, and design functionality is verified against the test plan.
6. **Software Bring-Up:** Real firmware, drivers, OS, and application code are run on the prototype to validate system-level behaviour.

**2. Multi-FPGA Partitioning Challenges (3 marks):**
- **Pin Limitation:** Inter-FPGA pins are scarce (typically 1000–3000 per board). A large SoC may have 100,000+ internal signals; many must be **time-multiplexed**, introducing routing delay and reducing effective clock frequency.
- **Timing Closure:** Partitioning introduces long combinational paths through MUX logic, making it difficult to close timing at high clock rates.
- **Debug Visibility:** Internal signals are harder to probe in hardware; **virtual I/O** and **embedded logic analyzers** (e.g., Xilinx ChipScope, Intel SignalTap) are used.
- **Clock Domain Crossing:** Partitioning may split a single clock domain across multiple FPGAs, requiring careful CDC handling.

**3. Concluding statement (1 mark):** Despite these challenges, fast prototyping remains indispensable for **pre-silicon software development** and **system-level validation** of complex SoCs.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of primary inputs: $n = 24$
- Vector application time (simulation): $t_{\text{vec}} = 50\ \text{ns} = 50 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$
- FPGA clock frequency: $f_{\text{clk}} = 100\ \text{MHz}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the exhaustive test set size (1 mark):**
$$\vert \mathcal{T}_{\text{exh}} \vert = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ vectors}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the simulation test time (1 mark):**
$$T_{\text{sim}} = \vert \mathcal{T}_{\text{exh}} \vert \times t_{\text{vec}}$$

$$T_{\text{sim}} = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{sim}} \approx 0.839\ \text{seconds}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the prototype clock period (1 mark):**
$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} = 10\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the prototype execution time (1 mark):**
Assuming one vector per clock cycle on the prototype:
$$T_{\text{proto}} = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{proto}} \approx 0.168\ \text{seconds}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the speedup factor (1 mark):**
$$S = \frac{T_{\text{sim}}}{T_{\text{proto}}} = \frac{0.839}{0.168} \approx 5\times$$

**Step 6 — Practical feasibility comment (2 marks):**
- **For the 24-input multiplier, 100% testing is PRACTICAL** because the total simulation time of ~0.84 seconds is negligible. The exhaustive approach guarantees *zero functional escape* and is feasible to deploy in the verification flow.
- **The FPGA prototype offers only a modest 5× speedup** for raw vector application. Its *true* advantage is not the test-vector speedup but the ability to run **real software workloads** (e.g., a cryptographic library, a video codec) at hardware speed, providing system-level validation that simulation cannot match.
- **Conclusion:** For a 24-input block, exhaustive testing is feasible and recommended for safety-critical multipliers. For the full SoC surrounding this multiplier, fast prototyping on FPGA is the appropriate choice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **layered verification methodology** used in modern VLSI design flow. Clearly indicate the role of Fast Prototyping and 100% Testing at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety-critical automotive IC has $N_{\text{total}} = 500{,}000$ potential stuck-at faults. Two verification strategies are compared:
  - **Strategy X (Simulation-based):** Detects $N_{\text{det,X}} = 475{,}000$ faults in 2 hours.
  - **Strategy Y (100% Testing):** Detects all $N_{\text{det,Y}} = 500{,}000$ faults in 8 hours.

If each undetected fault has a field-defect probability of $p = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ and the chip is shipped in a volume of $V = 5 \times 10^6$ units, compute the DPM (Defects Per Million) for both strategies. Justify which strategy is appropriate for an **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** automotive design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Layered Verification Methodology (7 marks)

The modern VLSI verification flow is structured as a **pyramid of increasing abstraction and decreasing speed**:

1. **Block-Level Simulation (Base layer):** Individual modules (ALU, decoder, FIFO) are verified using **directed tests** for known corner cases and **constrained-random tests** for broader coverage. **100% testing is applied to small critical blocks** (e.g., a 16-bit CRC engine) where input space is exhaustively tractable.
2. **Subsystem Verification (Mid layer):** Reusable **UVM/SystemVerilog** testbenches validate inter-block communication, protocols (AXI, AHB, PCIe), and data integrity. Constrained-random with **functional coverage** drives the verification.
3. **Full-Chip Simulation:** Integration-level regressions test the entire SoC over millions of cycles, often running for days across server farms.
4. **Emulation / FPGA Prototyping (Pre-silicon):** The complete SoC runs on multi-FPGA platforms at MHz speeds. **Fast Prototyping** is the dominant methodology at this layer, enabling real-software execution, OS boot, and stress testing.
5. **Post-Silicon Validation (Top layer):** Once fabricated, the chip is validated in the lab using ATE (Automatic Test Equipment) and bench testing.

A clean ASCII diagram representation:

```
        +-------------------------------+
        |  POST-SILICON VALIDATION      |   <-- Real silicon
        +-------------------------------+
        |  EMULATION / FAST PROTOTYPING |   <-- FPGA / Emulator
        +-------------------------------+
        |  FULL-CHIP SIMULATION         |   <-- Server farm, weeks
        +-------------------------------+
        |  SUBSYSTEM VERIFICATION       |   <-- UVM, days
        +-------------------------------+
        |  BLOCK-LEVEL SIMULATION       |   <-- 100% testing here
        +-------------------------------+
        |  RTL DESIGN                   |
        +-------------------------------+
```

**Role of 100% Testing:** Used only at the **base layer** for small critical blocks (typically $n \leq 25$ inputs).

**Role of Fast Prototyping:** Used at the **upper layers** for system-level, software-driven validation.

---

### Part (b) — DPM Calculation and ISO 26262 Justification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Fault coverage of Strategy X (1 mark):**
$$FC_X = \frac{475{,}000}{500{,}000} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

**Step 2 — Number of undetected faults in Strategy X (1 mark):**
$$N_{\text{undet,X}} = 500{,}000 - 475{,}000 = 25{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Expected defective units in Strategy X (1 mark):**
$$N_{\text{defect,X}} = N_{\text{undet,X}} \times p \times V$$
$$N_{\text{defect,X}} = 25{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^6 = 250{,}000$$

**Step 4 — DPM for Strategy X (1 mark):**
$$\text{DPM}_X = \frac{N_{\text{defect,X}}}{V} \times 10^6 = \frac{250{,}000}{5 \times 10^6} \times 10^6 = 50\ \text{DPM}$$

**Step 5 — DPM for Strategy Y (1 mark):**
Since Strategy Y detects all faults:
$$N_{\text{undet,Y}} = 0 \implies \text{DPM}_Y = 0$$

**Step 6 — ISO 26262 ASIL-D Justification (2 marks):**
- **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** is the **highest automotive safety integrity level**, applicable to systems whose failure could cause **life-threatening or fatal injury** without a margin for safety (e.g., brake-by-wire, airbag controllers, electric power steering).
- The **target DPM for ASIL-D** is typically **≤ 1 DPM** (and ideally zero).
- **Strategy X yields 50 DPM**, which is **50× above the ASIL-D threshold** and is therefore **NOT acceptable** for safety-critical deployment.
- **Strategy Y yields 0 DPM** through 100% testing, satisfying the ASIL-D safety goal, despite the longer (8-hour) test time.
- **Conclusion:** For ASIL-D automotive ICs, **Strategy Y (100% testing)** is the only acceptable approach. The extra 6 hours of test time is a small price to pay for guaranteed functional safety. In practice, the safety-critical blocks are partitioned to be small enough (n ≤ 25) for exhaustive testing to be feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Converting $50\ \text{ns}$ to seconds as $50 \times 10^{-9}$ is correct; writing $50 \times 10^{9}$ will give a wildly wrong answer and cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing n and N:** In $2^n$, $n$ is the *number of inputs*, not the *total fault count*. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting to justify:** In Part (b) numerical questions, KTU examiners award at least 1 mark for the **practical feasibility comment**. Skipping it loses a mark.
> 4. **DPM Definition:** Students often write DPM as a percentage. DPM is **per million**, not percent. Use the formula carefully.
> 5. **ISO 26262 Levels:** ASIL-A, B, C, D are *not* interchangeable. ASIL-D is the strictest. Mention the standard specifically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Fast Prototyping** = RTL → FPGA/Emulator realization for real-time, system-level validation.
- ☐ **100% Testing** = Application of all $2^n$ input vectors for combinational / $2^{n+m}$ for sequential circuits.

### Key Formulas
- ☐ Exhaustive test size: $\vert \mathcal{T}_{\text{exh}} \vert = 2^n$ (combinational), $2^{n+m}$ (sequential).
- ☐ Test time: $T_{\text{test}} = \vert \mathcal{T} \vert \times t_{\text{vec}}$.
- ☐ Fault coverage: $FC = \dfrac{N_{\text{det}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ DPM: $\text{DPM} = \dfrac{N_{\text{escape}}}{V} \times 10^6$.

### Critical Thresholds
- ☐ $n \leq 25$: Exhaustive testing is **practical**.
- ☐ $n \geq 30$: Exhaustive testing is **infeasible**; use Fast Prototyping + random/constrained verification.
- ☐ $n = 40$ at $1\ \mu s$/vec: ~12.7 days. $n = 64$: ~583,000 years.
- ☐ Industry DPM target: **≤ 1 DPM** for safety-critical ICs.

### Application Domains
- ☐ **Fast Prototyping → SoC pre-silicon, software bring-up, system validation.**
- ☐ **100% Testing → Safety-critical blocks (ISO 26262, IEC 62304, DO-254).**

### Key Industry Tools & Vendors
- ☐ Synopsys **HAPS** (FPGA-based prototyping).
- ☐ Cadence **Palladium** (emulation).
- ☐ Siemens **Veloce** (emulation).
- ☐ Xilinx **Vivado**, Intel **Quartus** (FPGA synthesis).

### Exam-Ready Buzzwords (Use in Answers for Higher Marks)
- ☐ "Combinatorial explosion"
- ☐ "Time-multiplexed cut signals"
- ☐ "Layered verification pyramid"
- ☐ "Functional coverage-driven closure"
- ☐ "Pre-silicon software validation"
- ☐ "Hardware-software co-verification"
- ☐ "Exhaustive vs. sampled coverage trade-off"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FPGA Prototyping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** FPGA Prototyping # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FPGA Prototyping** is the methodology of mapping a Register-Transfer Level (RTL) design—originally targeted for an **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)** or **System-on-Chip (SoC)**—onto a Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)** platform in order to achieve high-speed, near-real-time functional verification, firmware/software co-development, and architectural validation **prior to silicon fabrication**. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEEVT635 (VLSI Verification & Testing)** syllabus, FPGA prototyping is classified as a **hardware-assisted verification** technique that sits between pure software simulation (slow, exhaustive) and full ASIC emulation/emulation (expensive, complex). It bridges the **verification gap** that occurs when simulation becomes impractical for billion-gate designs. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Blueprint-to-Model-Home Workflow** > > Imagine an architect who has designed a 50-storey skyscraper (your final ASIC). Before pouring the foundation (taping out the silicon at ~$2–5 million for a modern node), the architect builds a **scaled-down, fully functional model home on a flexible wooden platform** (the FPGA). You can walk through it, test the elevators, validate plumbing, and even let residents try out the kitchen. > > The model is **not identical** to the skyscraper—it's built with different materials, has fewer floors, and runs slightly slower—but it is **behaviourally faithful enough** to catch 90% of design bugs at a fraction of the cost and time. If a pipe bursts in the model, you'll know to fix it before pouring concrete in the real building. > > **FPGA = the wooden model platform. ASIC = the final skyscraper.** ## 1.3 Why FPGA Prototyping? — The Verification Pyramid FPGA prototyping occupies a critical niche in the modern verification landscape. Below is the **verification effort vs. throughput trade-off**: | Technique | Speed | Capacity | Cost | Best Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | Software Simulation (HDL) | ~1 kHz | Unlimited (host-bound) | Low | Bug-hunting, coverage closure | | **FPGA Prototyping** | **1–100 MHz** | **10M–500M gates** | **Medium** | **Software bring-up, system validation** | | Hardware Emulation | 100 kHz – 2 MHz | 1B+ gates | Very High | Full-chip bring-up, low-power validation | | Silicon Bring-up | 100 MHz – 1+ GHz | Final silicon | Highest | Post-tapeout validation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** The module-level distinction often tested is that **FPGA prototyping ≠ FPGA-based emulation**. Prototyping favours **speed and early software development**, while emulation favours **internal visibility and debug**. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Key industry-standard metrics used throughout FPGA prototyping literature: - **Clock Frequency (F_max):** typically **100–500 MHz** on modern FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx Versal, Intel Stratix 10). - **Logic Capacity:** measured in **LUTs (Look-Up Tables)** and **ASIC-gate equivalents** (1 LUT ≈ 5–15 ASIC gates depending on architecture). - **Compile Time:** **2–24 hours** for a modern SoC, dominated by place-and-route. - **Cost per prototype board:** **$500 – $50,000** for commercial prototyping platforms (e.g., Xilinx VPK120, S2C Prodigy). - **Speed-up over simulation:** **1000× – 100,000×** depending on testbench complexity. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The FPGA Architecture — Building Blocks Modern FPGAs are heterogeneous platforms. Understanding their internal fabric is **mandatory** for prototyping because every ASIC RTL construct must map onto one of these primitives. ### 2.1.1 Configurable Logic Block (CLB / Logic Element) - **LUT (Look-Up Table):** An $n$-input LUT implements **any Boolean function of $n$ variables** by storing the truth table in SRAM. - A 6-input LUT has $2^6 = 64$ SRAM bits. - Modern FPGAs (Xilinx UltraScale+, Intel Agilex) use **6-input / 8-input fracturable LUTs**. - **Flip-Flop (FF):** Each LUT is paired with one or two edge-triggered D-FFs for sequential logic. - **Carry Chain:** Dedicated fast-propagation logic for arithmetic (adders, counters). ### 2.1.2 Memory Resources - **Block RAM (BRAM):** Dual-port SRAM blocks, typically **18 Kb or 36 Kb** each, configurable as single/dual port, FIFO, or shift register. - **Distributed RAM / LUT-RAM:** Small memories synthesized using LUTs themselves. - **UltraRAM (Xilinx):** **288 Kb** ultra-large blocks for high-capacity buffering. ### 2.1.3 DSP Slices - Hard-wired multipliers, accumulators, and MAC units. - E.g., a single **DSP48E2 slice** = $25 \times 18$ multiplier + 48-bit accumulator + pre-adder. ### 2.1.4 Clock Management - **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** and **MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager)** for frequency synthesis, phase shifting, and jitter filtering. - **Global clock networks** with low-skew distribution trees. ### 2.1.5 I/O & Transceivers - **General-purpose I/O (GPIO)** with programmable standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, etc.). - **High-Speed Serial Transceivers** up to **28 Gbps – 112 Gbps PAM4** for SerDes prototyping. ## 2.2 The FPGA Prototyping Flow — Step-by-Step > [!NOTE] > **Why this flow matters in KTU exams:** You will be asked to draw or describe this flow. Memorize the **order** and the **purpose** of each stage. ### Stage 1: RTL Preparation - RTL is assumed **synthesizable, synchronous, and free of simulation-only constructs** (`initial` blocks, `real` types, `$display`, etc.). - DFT (Design-for-Test) structures such as X-propagation must be addressed. ### Stage 2: Synthesis (Logic Synthesis) - Tool: **Xilinx Vivado, Synopsys Synplify, Intel Quartus, Yosys (open-source)**. - Maps RTL → gate-level netlist targeted to FPGA primitives. - **Resource estimation** is reported: LUT count, FF count, BRAM count, DSP count, I/O count. ### Stage 3: Partitioning (for multi-FPGA prototypes) - Large designs that exceed a single FPGA's capacity are **partitioned** across multiple FPGAs. - Partitioning strategy: **minimize inter-FPGA cuts**, maximize internal connectivity. ### Stage 4: Implementation (Place & Route) - Maps netlist onto physical FPGA fabric. - **Timing-driven P&R** closes timing against a target F_max (e.g., 100 MHz). - Critical step: balancing **timing closure vs. compile time**. ### Stage 5: Bitstream Generation - Produces a `.bit` (Xilinx) or `.sof` (Intel) file that configures FPGA SRAM cells. ### Stage 6: Board Bring-Up & Debug - Load bitstream onto the FPGA board. - Use **Integrated Logic Analyzers (ILA / ChipScope / SignalTap)** for internal signal visibility. - Run real-world workloads: firmware boot, OS bring-up (Linux on RISC-V/ARM), peripheral interfacing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **LUT storage capacity** | $N = 2^n$ bits | $n$ = number of LUT inputs | A 6-input LUT stores 64 bits | | 2 | **Setup time constraint** | $T_{clk} \geq T_{co} + T_{logic} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$ | $T_{co}$ = clock-to-out, $T_{logic}$ = combinational delay, $T_{su}$ = FF setup, $T_{skew}$ = clock skew | Determines $F_{max} = 1/T_{clk}$ | | 3 | **Maximum operating frequency** | $F_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}}$ | $T_{clk,min}$ = minimum clock period | Practical FPGAs: 100–500 MHz | | 4 | **ASIC-gate equivalent** | $\text{Gates} \approx k \times \text{LUTs}$ | $k \approx 5$–$15$ | Architecture-dependent constant | | 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = F_{clk} \times W_{data}$ | $W_{data}$ = data width in bits/lane | For streaming interfaces | | 6 | **Dynamic power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = load capacitance, $V$ = supply, $f$ = frequency | Quadratic in voltage | | 7 | **BRAM depth** | $\text{Depth} = \dfrac{\text{Total bits}}{W \times P}$ | $W$ = data width, $P$ = parity overhead | Configure for 18/36 Kb blocks | | 8 | **Multi-FPGA partitioning cost** | $C_{cut} = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} w(e)$ | $E_{cut}$ = edges crossing FPGAs | Minimize using Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm | | 9 | **Logic utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Used LUTs}}{\text{Available LUTs}} \times 100\%$ | — | Target 70–80% for timing closure headroom | | 10 | **Speedup over simulation** | $S = \dfrac{T_{FPGA}}{T_{sim}}$ | — | Typically $10^3$–$10^5$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility FPGA prototyping is the **de-facto industry standard** for: - **Mobile SoCs** (Qualcomm, Apple, Samsung) — pre-silicon firmware development. - **Automotive ECU validation** (ISO 26262 functional safety pre-silicon). - **AI/ML accelerator validation** — TensorRT/CUDA kernels before taping out custom NPU silicon. - **Networking ASICs** (Broadcom, Marvell) — 400G/800G packet processing validation. - **Aerospace & Defence** — radiation-tolerant design validation via FPGA emulation. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LUT Capacity vs. Boolean Function Coverage **Problem:** Show that a 4-input LUT can implement any Boolean function of 4 variables, and compute the minimum number of such LUTs needed to implement a 32-bit ripple-carry adder. ### Step 1 — Truth Table Representation A Boolean function of $n$ variables has $2^n$ possible input combinations. To fully specify the function, we need $2^n$ output values, each stored as one SRAM bit in the LUT. $$N_{bits} = 2^n$$ ### Step 2 — Apply to n = 4 $$N_{bits} = 2^4 = 16 \text{ SRAM bits}$$ This means a 4-input LUT can store any 4-input truth table — hence it can implement **any** 4-input Boolean function. ### Step 3 — Count Full-Adder Logic A 1-bit full adder has the following equations: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum} &= A \oplus B \oplus C_{in} \\ C_{out} &= (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B)) \end{aligned}$$ - The **Sum** function is 3-input → fits in **one 4-input LUT**. - The **Carry-out** function is 3-input → fits in **one 4-input LUT**. So each full-adder bit requires **2 LUTs**. ### Step 4 — Extrapolate to 32-bit Adder For a 32-bit ripple-carry adder: $$N_{LUTs} = 32 \times 2 = 64 \text{ LUTs}$$ ### Step 5 — Account for Carry Chain Modern FPGAs use **dedicated carry chains** that bypass LUTs for the propagate/generate logic. The actual LUT count is therefore lower: $$N_{LUTs, actual} \approx 32 \text{ LUTs (for Sum only)} + \text{carry logic overhead}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why FPGA prototyping is so efficient for arithmetic-heavy designs — the dedicated carry chain hardware gives **near-ASIC-equivalent performance** for adders and counters. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Timing Closure and Maximum Frequency **Problem:** Given the following path delays, compute the maximum operating frequency for an FPGA prototype. | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $T_{co}$ (clock-to-out) | 0.5 ns | | $T_{logic}$ (combinational logic) | 6.2 ns | | $T_{su}$ (setup time) | 0.3 ns | | $T_{skew}$ (clock skew) | 0.2 ns | ### Step 1 — Write the Setup-Time Inequality For a flip-flop-to-flip-flop path to function correctly: $$T_{clk} \geq T_{co} + T_{logic} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values $$\begin{aligned} T_{clk,min} &= 0.5 \text{ ns} + 6.2 \text{ ns} + 0.3 \text{ ns} + 0.2 \text{ ns} \\ &= 7.2 \text{ ns} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Maximum Frequency $$F_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \dfrac{1}{7.2 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 138.89 \text{ MHz}$$ ### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation - If the design target is 100 MHz, the path **meets timing** with margin: $(138.89 - 100)/138.89 = 28\%$ slack. - If the target is 150 MHz, the path **fails timing** by $150 - 138.89 = 11.11$ MHz. - **Action:** Use **pipelining**, **register retiming**, or **logic duplication** to reduce $T_{logic}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-FPGA Partitioning (Fiduccia-Mattheyses Bound) **Problem:** A 50M-gate ASIC must be partitioned across 4 identical FPGAs, each with a 1,500-pin I/O budget. Estimate the minimum cut-cost if the design has 250,000 internal nets and we assume 60% of nets can be kept internal. ### Step 1 — Define Cut-Cost $$C_{cut} = \sum_{e \in E_{cut}} w(e)$$ where $E_{cut}$ is the set of nets crossing FPGA boundaries. ### Step 2 — Calculate Crossing Nets If 60% of nets stay internal, then 40% cross: $$N_{cut} = 0.40 \times 250{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ nets}$$ ### Step 3 — Verify Pin Budget Assuming each crossing net consumes **one FPGA pin per endpoint** (and there are 2 endpoints): $$N_{pins,used} = 100{,}000 \times 2 = 200{,}000 \text{ pins}$$ Total available: $4 \times 1{,}500 = 6{,}000 \text{ pins}$. ### Step 4 — Analyse the Constraint Violation $$200{,}000 \gg 6{,}000$$ The naive cut is **33× over budget**. This means the design **must use time-multiplexing** (TDM) of pins. ### Step 5 — Apply TDM Multiplexing Ratio With a TDM ratio of $R$: $$R = \dfrac{200{,}000}{6{,}000} \approx 33.3$$ This means each pin carries **33 sequential frames**. In practice, modern prototyping platforms use $R = 4$ to $R = 16$, requiring intelligent **partitioning with multiple iterations of the FM algorithm** to reduce the cut. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **multi-FPGA prototyping introduces a TDM latency penalty** of $R$ clock cycles per crossing. This is a common exam trap. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ASIC vs. FPGA Trade-Off | Parameter | ASIC | FPGA Prototype | Implication | |---|---|---|---| | NRE cost | $2M–$500M+ | $0 (reprogrammable) | FPGA wins for low-volume | | Unit cost (volume) | $0.10–$50 | $50–$50,000 | ASIC wins for >100K units | | Performance | 1–5 GHz typical | 100–500 MHz typical | ASIC ~5–10× faster | | Power efficiency | Best-in-class | 5–15× worse | ASIC wins | | Time-to-prototype | 6–18 months | 1–4 weeks | FPGA wins by 100× | | Capacity | Unlimited | 10M–500M gates | ASIC wins | | Internal visibility | None (post-silicon only) | Full (ILA / ChipScope) | FPGA wins | | Functional safety validation | Late | Early | FPGA enables ISO 26262 pre-sil | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 FPGA Prototyping End-to-End Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RTL Design
VHDL / Verilog / SystemVerilog] --> B[RTL Linting & CDC Checks] B --> C[Synthesis
Vivado / Quartus / Synplify] C --> D{Design Fits
Single FPGA?} D -- Yes --> E[Place and Route] D -- No --> F[Multi-FPGA Partitioning
FM Algorithm] F --> E E --> G[Timing Closure
Static Timing Analysis] G --> H[Bitstream Generation
.bit or .sof file] H --> I[FPGA Board Bring-Up
JTAG / PCIe Load] I --> J[Debug with ILA
SignalTap / ChipScope] J --> K[Software Co-Development
Firmware / OS / Drivers] K --> L[System Validation
Real-World Workloads] L --> M{All Tests Pass?} M -- Yes --> N[Tape-Out to Foundry
ASIC Fabrication] M -- No --> O[Bug Report to RTL Team] O --> A ``` ## 4.2 FPGA Internal Architecture (Modern Heterogeneous SoC FPGA) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FPGA[Modern FPGA Fabric] CLB[Configurable Logic Blocks
LUTs and Flip Flops] BRAM[Block RAM
18 Kb / 36 Kb] DSP[DSP Slices
Multipliers and MACs] URAM[UltraRAM
288 Kb Blocks] MMCM[Clock Management
PLL / MMCM] IO[Programmable I/O
LVCMOS / LVDS / SerDes] PCIE[Hard IP Cores
PCIe Gen5 / 100G Ethernet] ARM[Hard Processor Subsystem
ARM Cortex-A53 / R5] end CLB <--> BRAM CLB <--> DSP CLB <--> MMCM BRAM <--> URAM CLB --> IO DSP --> IO PCIE --> IO ARM --> CLB ARM --> BRAM ``` ## 4.3 Multi-FPGA Partitioning Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph FullDesign[Full ASIC Design
50M Gates] Core1[Core 1
CPU Subsystem] Core2[Core 2
Memory Controller] Core3[Core 3
GPU / NPU] Core4[Core 4
Peripherals and I/O] end Core1 --> FPGA1[FPGA Board 1
Logic + BRAM + DSP] Core2 --> FPGA2[FPGA Board 2
Logic + BRAM + DSP] Core3 --> FPGA3[FPGA Board 3
Logic + BRAM + DSP] Core4 --> FPGA4[FPGA Board 4
Logic + BRAM + DSP] FPGA1 <-- High-Speed Connectors
TDM Pins --> FPGA2 FPGA2 <-- High-Speed Connectors
TDM Pins --> FPGA3 FPGA3 <-- High-Speed Connectors
TDM Pins --> FPGA4 FPGA1 <-- Debug and Trace --> HostPC[Host PC
JTAG / PCIe] ``` ## 4.4 Verification Coverage Flow — From Simulation to Prototyping ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Block-Level Simulation] --> B[Sub-System Simulation] B --> C[Full-Chip Simulation
Slow, Exhaustive] C --> D[FPGA Prototyping
Fast, Near-Real-Time] D --> E[Hardware Emulation
High Visibility] E --> F[Silicon Bring-Up
Final Validation] F --> G[Volume Production] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define FPGA prototyping. List any four advantages of using FPGA prototyping for ASIC/SoC verification.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** FPGA prototyping is the process of implementing a Register-Transfer Level (RTL) design (originally targeted for ASIC fabrication) onto a Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) Field-Programmable Gate Array platform to perform high-speed functional verification, software co-development, and architectural validation before committing to silicon fabrication. **Four Advantages:** 1. **High speed:** Operates at 10–100 MHz vs. ~kHz for software simulation, enabling real-world workload execution. 2. **Early software bring-up:** Allows firmware, drivers, OS, and even application code to be developed and validated before silicon is ready. 3. **Low cost:** FPGA boards cost $500–$50,000 versus $2M+ ASIC NRE — ideal for low-to-medium volume validation. 4. **Full internal visibility:** Tools like Xilinx ILA and Intel SignalTap allow real-time capture of internal signals without modifying the design. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between FPGA prototyping and hardware emulation. State one scenario where each is preferred.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | FPGA Prototyping | Hardware Emulation | |---|---|---| | **Speed** | 10–100 MHz | 100 kHz – 2 MHz | | **Capacity** | 10M–500M gates | 1B+ gates | | **Cost** | $500 – $50,000 | $1M – $10M | | **Visibility** | Limited (ILA) | Full (every signal observable) | | **Compile time** | 2–24 hours | Hours to days | | **Use case** | Software bring-up, system validation | Full-chip debug, low-power validation | - **FPGA prototyping preferred:** Early firmware development for a mobile SoC, where speed matters more than signal visibility. - **Hardware emulation preferred:** Power-aware verification of a billion-gate server CPU, where exhaustive internal-state observation is required. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the internal architecture of a modern FPGA. Discuss the role of LUTs, Block RAMs, DSP slices, and clock management blocks in prototyping an ASIC design. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A modern FPGA is a **heterogeneous programmable fabric** containing the following key resources: **1. Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs) / Logic Elements (LEs):** - Each CLB contains multiple **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** that implement arbitrary Boolean functions. - An $n$-input LUT stores $2^n$ SRAM bits representing the function's truth table. - A 6-input LUT can implement any 6-variable Boolean function directly, or two independent functions of fewer inputs (fracturable LUT). - Each LUT is paired with one or two **flip-flops** for sequential storage. - During ASIC prototyping, RTL flip-flops, state machines, and pipeline registers are inferred and mapped to these CLB flip-flops. - **Carry-chain logic** within CLBs accelerates arithmetic operations like adders, subtractors, and counters — critical for prototyping DSP-heavy ASICs. **2. Block RAM (BRAM):** - Dedicated dual-port SRAM blocks, typically **18 Kb or 36 Kb** per instance. - Configurable as single-port, dual-port, or FIFO with independent read/write clocks. - Used to prototype ASIC SRAMs, register files, FIFOs, packet buffers, and lookup tables. - E.g., Xilinx UltraScale+ offers **UltraRAM blocks of 288 Kb** for large memory arrays. **3. DSP Slices:** - Hard-wired multipliers (e.g., 27 × 18 or 25 × 18) combined with pre-adders, accumulators, and pipeline registers. - A single Xilinx **DSP48E2** can perform a MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operation in one clock cycle. - Essential for prototyping **AI accelerators, FIR filters, FFT engines**, and other compute-intensive blocks. **4. Clock Management Tiles (CMT):** - Comprises **MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager)** and **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** blocks. - Generate multiple clock frequencies from a single reference oscillator. - Provide fine-grained phase shifting, frequency synthesis, and jitter filtering. - Global clock distribution networks offer **low-skew** propagation across the entire fabric. **5. I/O Blocks & Transceivers:** - Programmable I/O supporting standards like LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, HSTL. - High-speed **SerDes transceivers** (up to 28–112 Gbps PAM4) for prototyping multi-gigabit serial interfaces like PCIe Gen5, 100G/400G Ethernet, and Interlaken. **Synthesis Note for Prototyping:** All RTL constructs in the ASIC must be **inferable** as one of these primitives. Vendor-supplied IP cores (memory controllers, PCIe, Ethernet MACs) further accelerate prototyping. --- **(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain the complete FPGA prototyping flow from RTL handoff to board-level system validation. Identify the major challenges at each stage. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Flowchart** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram). **Stage-by-Stage Explanation with Challenges:** | Stage | Activity | Major Challenge | |---|---|---| | **1. RTL Preparation** | Ensure synthesizable, synchronous RTL | Removing X-propagation, handling asynchronous resets, eliminating simulation-only constructs (`initial`, `$display`, `real`). | | **2. Synthesis** | Map RTL → FPGA gate-level netlist | Resource over-utilization; inferring BRAM/DSP correctly; clock-domain crossing (CDC) issues. | | **3. Partitioning** (multi-FPGA) | Split design across multiple FPGAs | Minimizing inter-FPGA cut-cost; pin budget exhaustion; TDM latency. | | **4. Place & Route** | Physical mapping onto fabric | Timing closure failures at target F_max; routing congestion. | | **5. Timing Closure** | Static Timing Analysis (STA) | Long combinational paths, clock skew, hold-time violations. | | **6. Bitstream Gen** | Produce configuration file | Long compile times (up to 24 hours); bitstream encryption for IP protection. | | **7. Board Bring-Up** | Load bitstream, verify operation | Power sequencing issues; configuration failures; PCB signal-integrity problems. | | **8. Debug** | Use ILA / SignalTap | Limited trace depth; storage of probe data; correlating with software events. | | **9. Software Co-Development** | Boot firmware, OS, drivers | Memory-map mismatches between FPGA prototype and final ASIC; clock-rate differences. | | **10. System Validation** | Real-world workload testing | I/O timing differences vs. ASIC; inability to model silicon-specific effects (e.g., on-chip variation). | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by failing to mention **clock-domain crossing (CDC) handling** and **X-propagation** as mandatory RTL preparation steps. A prototype with X's propagating through flip-flops gives **misleading results**. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** A 60M-gate ASIC is to be prototyped on four FPGAs, each with a 1,200-pin I/O budget. The design contains 280,000 internal nets, of which 65% can be absorbed internally within each FPGA partition. Assuming a 4:1 time-division multiplexing (TDM) ratio is applied to crossing nets, compute: **(i)** the number of nets crossing FPGA boundaries, **(ii)** the total pin utilization per FPGA, and **(iii)** the latency penalty introduced by TDM if the system clock is 100 MHz. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Total nets = 280,000 - Internal nets (no cut) = 65% of 280,000 = 182,000 - External (crossing) nets = 35% of 280,000 - TDM ratio = 4:1 - I/O budget per FPGA = 1,200 pins - $F_{clk}$ = 100 MHz **(i) Number of Crossing Nets:** $$N_{cut} = 0.35 \times 280{,}000 = 98{,}000 \text{ nets}$$ **(ii) Pin Utilization per FPGA:** Each crossing net has two endpoints (one source, one sink across FPGAs): $$N_{pins, raw} = 98{,}000 \times 2 = 196{,}000 \text{ pins}$$ After applying 4:1 TDM (each pin carries 4 logical nets): $$N_{pins, effective} = \dfrac{196{,}000}{4} = 49{,}000 \text{ pins distributed across 4 FPGAs}$$ $$N_{pins, per FPGA} = \dfrac{49{,}000}{4} = 12{,}250 \text{ pins per FPGA}$$ **Pin utilization per FPGA:** $$U_{pin} = \dfrac{12{,}250}{1{,}200} \times 100\% = 1020.83\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Finding:** Pin utilization exceeds the budget by **over 10×**. This design is **NOT prototypable** on the given hardware configuration. Either: (a) increase TDM ratio to ≥11, (b) use a higher-pin-count FPGA board, or (c) reduce design size via abstraction. **(iii) TDM Latency Penalty:** $$\begin{aligned} T_{clk} &= \dfrac{1}{F_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns} \\ T_{TDM} &= R \times T_{clk} = 4 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 40 \text{ ns} \end{aligned}$$ The TDM fabric introduces a **40 ns latency per inter-FPGA crossing**. For designs with deep inter-FPGA pipelines, this can become the dominant bottleneck. --- **(b)** Discuss the major limitations of FPGA prototyping. How do these limitations influence design choices during the RTL coding phase? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Major Limitations of FPGA Prototyping:** **1. Capacity Mismatch:** - Modern FPGAs offer 10M–500M ASIC-gate equivalents; large SoCs exceed single-device capacity. - Forces multi-FPGA partitioning, adding cost and complexity. **2. Timing & Speed Differences:** - FPGAs run at 100–500 MHz vs. ASICs at 1–5 GHz. - FPGA LUT delays and routing delays are not representative of final silicon timing. - Static timing analysis on FPGA does **not** predict ASIC silicon performance. **3. I/O and Pin Limitations:** - Limited physical I/O pins (typically 100–2,000 per FPGA) vs. unlimited ASIC pads. - Requires TDM, which introduces latency. **4. Resource Heterogeneity:** - Hard macros (BRAM, DSP) are fixed-size; e.g., an 18 Kb BRAM cannot be efficiently used for a 10 Kb memory. - Custom ASIC IPs (analog blocks, PLLs, SERDES) often have no FPGA equivalent. **5. Limited Internal Visibility:** - ILA / SignalTap probes consume LUTs and BRAMs; trace depth is bounded (kilobytes per probe). - Cannot easily match the full visibility of hardware emulators. **6. Compile Time:** - Place-and-route for a 100M-gate design can take 12–24 hours, slowing iteration cycles. **Influence on RTL Coding Choices:** | Limitation | RTL Coding Adaptation | |---|---| | Capacity | Use **generics / parameters** to scale design; prefer resource sharing via time-multiplexing in RTL. | | Timing | Use **pipelining aggressively**; register every combinational path > 4 LUT levels. | | I/O | Avoid wide parallel external interfaces in RTL; prefer **serialized protocols** (AXI-Stream, Aurora). | | BRAM granularity | Round memory sizes to nearest FPGA-friendly depth (e.g., 1024, 2048, 4096). | | Visibility | Add **synthesis-time `mark_debug` attributes** to critical signals for ILA. | | Compile time | Use **incremental compilation** flows; partition RTL hierarchy early. | | Clock domains | Use **synchronizers, FIFOs, and CDC-safe structures** from the start — they must work on both FPGA and ASIC. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **FPGA limitations** with **ASIC limitations** in your answer. FPGA limitations affect **how you prepare the RTL**; ASIC limitations affect **what the final silicon can do**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — FPGA Prototyping** - **Definition:** FPGA prototyping maps ASIC RTL onto a COTS FPGA for fast pre-silicon verification. - **Speed advantage:** Operates 10–100 MHz, giving **1000×–100,000× speedup** over HDL simulation. - **Cost advantage:** $500–$50,000 vs. $2M+ ASIC NRE. - **Modern FPGA primitives:** LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP slices, MMCM/PLL, SerDes, hard IP cores (PCIe, ARM cores). - **LUT capacity:** An $n$-input LUT stores $2^n$ SRAM bits → can implement any $n$-input Boolean function. - **Timing closure:** Governed by $T_{clk} \geq T_{co} + T_{logic} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$. - **FPGA prototyping vs. emulation:** Prototyping → speed; Emulation → visibility. - **Multi-FPGA partitioning:** Use **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM)** algorithm to minimize cut-cost. - **TDM penalty:** Each inter-FPGA crossing incurs $R \times T_{clk}$ latency, where $R$ is the multiplexing ratio. - **Pin budget:** Critical constraint in multi-FPGA flows — calculate as $N_{pins} = (N_{cut} \times 2) / R$. - **RTL preparation mandatory:** Remove X-propagation, `$display`, `real` types, asynchronous resets where possible. - **Debug tools:** Xilinx **ILA**, Intel **SignalTap**, Synopsys **Identify** for internal visibility. - **Compile time:** 2–24 hours, dominated by place-and-route; use **incremental compilation**. - **Resource utilization:** Target 70–80% LUT utilization for timing closure headroom. - **Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — FPGA power is 5–15× worse than equivalent ASIC. - **Industrial impact:** Used by every major SoC vendor (Qualcomm, Apple, Samsung, Broadcom) for pre-silicon software bring-up. - **Functional safety:** Critical for ISO 26262 (automotive) and DO-254 (aerospace) pre-silicon validation. - **Difference from emulation:** Speed and cost trade-off — prototyping is the "budget" version with less visibility. - **Differences from simulation:** Speed trade-off — simulation is exhaustive but slow; prototyping is fast but not exhaustive. > [!NOTE] > **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: FPGA Prototyping** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PEEVT635 syllabus guidelines.*

topics.title: Emulation Based Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emulation Based Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emulation-Based Testing** is a dynamic verification methodology in which the Register-Transfer Level (RTL) design-under-test (DUT) is mapped onto a reconfigurable, multi-FPGA-based hardware platform called an *emulator*, which mimics the logical and temporal behavior of the final ASIC at speeds orders of magnitude faster than software simulation. The emulator executes the design in real-time (or near real-time) using a hardware model built from programmable logic elements, dedicated interconnect fabrics, and high-speed host workstations.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEVT635, Module 1), emulation is positioned as a *bridging verification strategy* between event-driven software simulation (slow, high visibility) and silicon prototyping (fast, low visibility).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Emulation is treated as a **co-emulation** technique in which the DUT (mapped in hardware) is exercised by a **testbench environment running on a workstation** that communicates via high-speed transactors or SCE-MI (Standard Co-Emulation Modeling Interface) channels.

### Key Industry Emulator Platforms (KTU Examinable Context)

| Platform | Vendor | Architecture Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Palladium** | Cadence | Custom-FPGA / Processor-based |
| **ZeBu** | Synopsys | Custom FPGA Array |
| **Veloce** | Siemens EDA (Mentor) | Custom FPGA Array |
| **Protium** | Cadence | FPGA-based prototyping |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examination purposes, students are expected to know the **conceptual architecture** of an emulator, not the vendor-specific tool features.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have designed a complex **electrical circuit diagram** for a building. Before laying real wires, you have two options:

- **Simulation** is like solving the circuit equations by hand on paper for every node, every clock tick — accurate but painstakingly slow.
- **Emulation** is like building a **physical miniature model** of the circuit using reconfigurable breadboards (FPGAs), wiring up the same logic, and then probing it with real multimeters. The model is not the *final silicon*, but it *behaves* exactly like the silicon at functional level, and you can test it at near real-time speeds by plugging in real peripherals (keyboard, display, network cable).

In essence, emulation **borrows hardware (FPGAs) to *mimic* silicon behavior** so that real-world software and I/O traffic can validate the design before tape-out.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory anchor:** *Emulation = Real hardware executing a real model of the design, driven by a real testbench, in real time.*

## 1.3 Why Emulation? The Verification Crisis

Modern SoCs contain **billions of transistors** and run embedded software stacks (Linux, drivers, protocol stacks) of millions of lines. Traditional simulation runs at ~**100 Hz to 1 kHz effective clock speed** when compiled, which is far too slow to boot an operating system. Emulation solves this by providing **MHz-to-tens-of-MHz** execution speed.

$$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{sim}}}{T_{\text{emu}}} \approx 10^3 \text{ to } 10^6 $$

where $T_{\text{sim}}$ is wall-clock simulation time and $T_{\text{emu}}$ is emulation time for the same stimulus set.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1 — Compilation & Partitioning
The RTL is first synthesized into a gate-level netlist, then **partitioned** across multiple FPGAs because a single FPGA cannot hold the entire design. The partitioning algorithm minimizes cut-size (number of signals crossing partition boundaries), since each cut becomes a routing bottleneck.

### Step 2 — Mapping & Place-and-Route
Each partition is mapped onto a physical FPGA (LUTs, BRAMs, DSPs, transceivers). Inter-FPGA signals are routed through the emulator's **interconnect fabric** (a backplane of wires, MGT channels, or dedicated Time-Division-Multiplexed links).

### Step 3 — Time-Multiplexing (TDM)
Because the inter-FPGA pin count is finite, multiple logical signals are **time-multiplexed** on a single physical wire. A TDM ratio of $R$ means $R$ internal cycles are compressed into 1 external cycle, costing effective speed:

$$ f_{\text{effective}} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{R} $$

### Step 4 — Testbench Co-Execution
The testbench (often SystemVerilog/UVM running on a workstation) drives the DUT via **transactors** that translate high-level transactions (e.g., AXI writes) into pin-level waveforms at the emulator boundary.

### Step 5 — Speed Adaptation
Emulator clock is typically **slower** than the real ASIC clock. The transactors use **clock and data recovery / stretching** to match.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** Emulation exists because software simulation cannot keep pace with the size of modern SoCs. The 'How' is to *parallelize* the design's execution by spreading it across thousands of FPGAs that all run in lockstep.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Speedup factor | $S$ | $S = T_{\text{sim}} / T_{\text{emu}}$ | dimensionless | Usually $10^3$–$10^6$ |
| Effective clock | $f_{\text{eff}}$ | $f_{\text{eff}} = f_{\text{FPGA}} / R$ | Hz | $R$ = TDM ratio |
| TDM ratio | $R$ | $R = N_{\text{logical}} / N_{\text{physical}}$ | integer | Higher $R$ = more muxing = slower |
| Inter-FPGA bandwidth required | $B_{\text{req}}$ | $B_{\text{req}} = S_{\text{cut}} \times f_{\text{eff}} \times w$ | bits/s | $S_{\text{cut}}$ = cut size, $w$ = signal width |
| Partition count | $P$ | $P = \lceil N_{\text{gates}} / N_{\text{FPGA}} \rceil$ | integer | $N_{\text{gates}}$ = total gate count |
| Emulation cost-time product | $C \times T$ | $C \times T = N_{\text{FPGA}} \times T_{\text{run}}$ | USD·s or FPGA·s | Used for capacity planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used inside the table instead of the pipe symbol `\|` to avoid breaking the markdown table. LaTeX renders $\vert$ as a clean vertical line for absolute value or conditional probability.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-silicon software development:** Mobile SoC teams boot Android/Linux on emulators months before silicon arrives.
- **Power estimation:** Activity-based power profiling is performed using toggle counts from emulated runs.
- **Hardware-software co-verification:** Firmware/driver validation in real CPU environments.
- **Compliance testing:** Protocols like USB, PCIe, DDR are exercised with real protocol analyzers.
- **Regression acceleration:** Regression suites that take weeks in simulation complete in hours.

## 2.4 Emulation vs Simulation vs FPGA Prototyping

| Attribute | Simulation | Emulation | FPGA Prototyping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | 100 Hz – 1 kHz | 0.5 – 5 MHz | 10 – 100 MHz |
| Visibility | Full waveform | Limited (probes only) | Very limited |
| Capacity | Unlimited (host RAM) | Hundreds of M-gates | Tens of M-gates |
| Debug | Excellent | Good (sampled) | Poor |
| Cost | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| Setup time | Minutes | Hours–days | Days–weeks |
| Use case | Block-level, corner cases | System-level, SW bring-up | System validation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Emulation-Based Testing" is a *methodological/architectural* topic rather than a pure math derivation, this section presents a **worked analytical example** showing how to compute key emulator-design trade-offs, following the KTU 2024 expectation of quantitative reasoning in design questions.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

A SoC design has a gate count of $N_{\text{gates}} = 120 \times 10^6$ gates. You are given an emulator with $P_{\text{FPGA}} = 8$ FPGAs, each capable of holding $C_{\text{FPGA}} = 20 \times 10^6$ gates. The inter-FPGA cut size is $S_{\text{cut}} = 5{,}000$ signals, each of width $w = 4$ bits. The FPGA runs at $f_{\text{FPGA}} = 100$ MHz, and the design's target clock is $f_{\text{target}} = 50$ MHz. Find:

1. Number of partitions $P$ required
2. TDM ratio $R$
3. Effective emulation clock $f_{\text{eff}}$
4. Required inter-FPGA bandwidth $B_{\text{req}}$
5. Whether real-time emulation is achievable

## 3.2 Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Compute the Number of Partitions

The number of partitions equals the total gate count divided by the per-FPGA capacity, rounded up.

$$
P = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{gates}}}{C_{\text{FPGA}}} \right\rceil
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
P = \left\lceil \frac{120 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^6} \right\rceil
$$

$$
P = \left\lceil 6 \right\rceil = 6
$$

**Result:** $P = 6$ partitions are required, which fits within the available 8 FPGAs (a $33\%$ capacity headroom).

### Step 2: Compute the TDM Ratio

The TDM ratio equals the total number of logical signals to be routed divided by the available physical channels. For a single inter-FPGA link, the number of logical signals per partition boundary is the cut size.

$$
R = \frac{S_{\text{cut}} \times w}{N_{\text{channel}}}
$$

Assuming each physical channel carries 1 bit per FPGA cycle and we have an effective channel width of $N_{\text{channel}} = 40$ bits per boundary:

$$
R = \frac{5000 \times 4}{40} = \frac{20{,}000}{40} = 500
$$

**Result:** $R = 500$. This means the FPGA's internal logic must be clocked 500× faster than the design's external interface to keep the boundary data flowing in real time.

### Step 3: Compute the Effective Emulation Clock

The effective emulation clock equals the FPGA clock divided by the TDM ratio.

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{R}
$$

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{500}
$$

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = 200{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 200 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Result:** $f_{\text{eff}} = 200 \text{ kHz}$.

### Step 4: Compute the Required Inter-FPGA Bandwidth

The bandwidth required is the cut size times the signal width times the effective frequency.

$$
B_{\text{req}} = S_{\text{cut}} \times w \times f_{\text{eff}}
$$

$$
B_{\text{req}} = 5000 \times 4 \times 200 \times 10^3
$$

$$
B_{\text{req}} = 20{,}000 \times 200{,}000
$$

$$
B_{\text{req}} = 4 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} = 4 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Result:** $B_{\text{req}} = 4$ Gbps.

### Step 5: Real-Time Achievability Check

For real-time emulation, the effective clock must equal or exceed the design's target clock.

$$
f_{\text{eff}} \stackrel{?}{\geq} f_{\text{target}}
$$

$$
200 \text{ kHz} \stackrel{?}{\geq} 50 \text{ MHz}
$$

Since $200{,}000 \text{ Hz} \ll 50{,}000{,}000 \text{ Hz}$, the condition fails.

**Result:** Real-time emulation is **NOT achievable** with this TDM configuration. To achieve real-time, we must either:

- Reduce cut size $S_{\text{cut}}$ (better partitioning)
- Increase $f_{\text{FPGA}}$ (faster FPGAs)
- Increase $N_{\text{channel}}$ (wider physical links)

### Step 6: Reverse-Solve Required TDM for Real-Time

Set $f_{\text{eff}} = f_{\text{target}}$ and solve for $R_{\max}$:

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{f_{\text{target}}} = \frac{100 \text{ MHz}}{50 \text{ MHz}} = 2
$$

This means the TDM ratio must be $\le 2$, requiring $N_{\text{channel}} \ge 10{,}000$ bits per boundary — a heavy interconnect demand. The designer would typically accept *slower-than-real-time* emulation (which is the practical reality) and rely on **transactors** to bridge the timing gap.

## 3.3 Verification of Numerical Consistency

Total signals across all boundaries:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = P \times S_{\text{cut}} = 6 \times 5{,}000 = 30{,}000
$$

Total bandwidth across the whole emulator:

$$
B_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{total}} \times w \times f_{\text{eff}} = 30{,}000 \times 4 \times 200 \text{ kHz} = 24 \text{ Gbps}
$$

This represents the *aggregate* bandwidth the interconnect fabric must sustain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `hostA`, `fpgaB`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node IDs.
- Node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric, no markdown bold/italics/HTML tables inside quotes.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Emulation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hostA["HOST WORKSTATION"]:::host
    tbA["SOFTWARE TESTBENCH UVM SYSTEMVERILOG"]:::tb
    sceA["SCE MI TRANSACTOR INTERFACE"]:::sce
    emuA["EMULATOR CHASSIS BACKPLANE FABRIC"]:::emu
    fpgaA["FPGA PARTITION 01 DUT SLICE"]:::fpga
    fpgaB["FPGA PARTITION 02 DUT SLICE"]:::fpga
    fpgaC["FPGA PARTITION 03 DUT SLICE"]:::fpga
    probeA["PROBE AND TRIGGER LOGIC"]:::probe
    dbgA["WAVEFORM DEBUG DATABASE"]:::dbg

    hostA --> tbA
    tbA --> sceA
    sceA -- "TX RX TRANSACTIONS" --> emuA
    emuA -- "TDM MULTIPLEXED I/O" --> fpgaA
    emuA -- "TDM MULTIPLEXED I/O" --> fpgaB
    emuA -- "TDM MULTIPLEXED I/O" --> fpgaC
    fpgaA -- "INTER FPGA LINKS" --> fpgaB
    fpgaB -- "INTER FPGA LINKS" --> fpgaC
    fpgaA --> probeA
    fpgaB --> probeA
    fpgaC --> probeA
    probeA --> dbgA
    dbgA --> hostA

    classDef host fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tb fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sce fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef emu fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fpga fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef probe fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dbg fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **host workstation** runs the UVM/SystemVerilog testbench at software speed.
2. Transactions are passed through the **SCE-MI transactor** to a portable, standardized C-API.
3. The **emulator backplane** demultiplexes and delivers the transactions to the appropriate **FPGA partition**.
4. The **inter-FPGA links** carry the design's internal signals (clock-domain crossings, pipeline stages) between slices.
5. **Probe logic** samples internal signals non-intrusively and streams them to a **debug database**.

## 4.3 Compilation and Runtime Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rtlA["RTL DESIGN VERILOG VHDL"]:::src
    synA["SYNTHESIS TO GATE LEVEL"]:::step
    partA["PARTITIONING ACROSS FPGAS"]:::step
    mapA["PLACE AND ROUTE ON FPGA"]:::step
    tdmA["TDM AND INTER FPGA MAPPING"]:::step
    exeA["RUNTIME EMULATION EXECUTION"]:::run
    chkA["CHECKER AND COVERAGE COLLECTION"]:::run
    rptA["REPORT GENERATION"]:::run

    rtlA --> synA --> partA --> mapA --> tdmA --> exeA --> chkA --> rptA

    classDef src fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef run fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Transaction-Level vs In-Circuit Emulation Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    modeA{"EMULATION MODE"}:::decision
    tlmA["TRANSACTION LEVEL EMULATION"]:::mode
    iceA["IN CIRCUIT EMULATION"]:::mode

    modeA -- "SOFTWARE TESTBENCH DRIVEN" --> tlmA
    modeA -- "REAL HARDWARE PERIPHERAL DRIVEN" --> iceA

    tlmA -- "STIMULUS" --> sceB["SCE MI TRANSACTOR"]:::sce
    sceB --> dutA["DUT IN EMULATOR"]:::dut

    iceA -- "REAL I/O" --> tgtA["TARGET BOARD WITH REAL PERIPHERALS"]:::target
    tgtA --> dutA

    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef mode fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sce fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dut fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Distinguish between **Transaction-Based Emulation** (uses transactors, runs at slow speed, ideal for protocol testing) and **In-Circuit Emulation** (uses real target boards via speed adapters, runs closer to real-time, ideal for HW/SW co-verification).
